old_claim.note: Continuous Integration provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Secure build, test, and deployment pipelines make CI/CD automation a key implementation surface for software supply-chain security, though the security practice also covers non-CI build workflows.

source_shape.source_locator: NIST SSDF groups practices for secure software development, build artifacts, deployment, and supply-chain risk mitigation across SDLC automation.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames software supply-chain security as secure-development and SDLC practice, including build and deployment artifacts.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Continuous integration is modeled as enabling because it supplies the automated build/test/deployment surface that SSDF-style controls protect, without being the only possible workflow.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: continuous_integration remains an enabling edge with review-level source support.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to policy-only supply-chain governance without build or deployment-pipeline controls.
A stronger source showed CI/CD automation is irrelevant to software supply-chain security practice.
A separate secure_build_pipelines node replaced continuous_integration as the more precise prerequisite.
short_reason: CI/CD automation is a key supply-chain security surface.
